perturbation.original_text: Each party shall use its reasonable best eﬀorts to keep the other's Conﬁdential Information secret. Each party shall use at least the same degree of care to avoid unauthorized disclosure or use of the other's Conﬁdential Information as it employs with respect to its own Conﬁdential Information of like importance.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall use commercially reasonable eﬀorts to keep the other's Conﬁdential Information secret. Each party shall use a degree of care that the disclosing party deems sufficient to avoid unauthorized disclosure or use of the other's Conﬁdential Information as it employs with respect to its own Conﬁdential Information of like importance.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'reasonable best efforts' to a 'degree of care that the disclosing party deems sufficient' reduces the standard of care and creates an unenforceable obligation, as the standard is set unilaterally. This also affects the duty to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: § 4 of the German Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. By allowing the disclosing party to define the level of protection, the contract may fall short of the 'reasonable measures' standard imposed by law, especially if the disclosing party sets a very low standard.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 13 MBPolVDVDV Gesamtergebnis des Auswahlverfahrens und Rangfolge der Bewerberinnen und Bewerber Verordnung buzer.de Bundesrecht - tagaktuell konsolidiert - alle Fassungen seit 2006 Vorschriftensuche § / Artikel Gesetz Volltextsuche nur in MBPolVDVDV ↑ nach oben ↓ nach unten Start Suchen Sachgebiete Aktuell Verkündet Über buzer.de Qualität Kontakt Datenschutz Impressum Tools: Blog-Plugin Mobilversion Vorschau Update via: Web-Widget Feed Rechtskataster Support Buzer.de Werben auf buzer.de Sie sind hier: Start > Inhaltsverzeichnis MBPolVDVDV > § 13 Mail bei Änderungen § 13 - Verordnung über den Vorbereitungsdienst für den mittleren Polizeivollzugsdienst in der Bundespolizei (MBPolVDVDV) V. v. 06.03.2020 BGBl. I S. 506 ( Nr. 13 ); zuletzt geändert durch Artikel 2 V. v. 15.12.2022 BGBl. I S. 2862 Geltung ab 01.03.2020; FNA: 2030-6-34 Beamte 2 weitere Fassungen | wird in 2 Vorschriften zitiert Teil 2 Auswahlverfahren und Einstellung § 12 ← → § 14 § 13 Gesamtergebnis des Auswahlverfahrens und Rangfolge der Bewerberinnen und Bewerber (1) Die Auswahlkommission ermittelt für jede Bewerberin und jeden Bewerber, die oder der den mündlichen Teil des Auswahlverfahrens bestanden hat, das Gesamtergebnis des Auswahlverfahrens. (2) In das Gesamtergebnis gehen die Ergebnisse der drei Teile des Auswahlverfahrens ein. (3) Anhand der Gesamtergebnisse legt die Bundespolizeiakademie eine Rangfolge der Bewerberinnen und Bewerber fest, die das Auswahlverfahren bestanden haben. (4) 1 Wer erfolglos am Auswahlverfahren teilgenommen hat, erhält eine schriftliche oder elektronische Mitteilung über die Ablehnung. 2 Die Bewerbungsunterlagen sind auf Wunsch zurückzusenden, ansonsten zu vernichten. 3 Elektronisch eingereichte Bewerbungsunterlagen sind endgültig zu löschen. § 12 ← Inhaltsverzeichnis → § 14 MBPolVDVDV - Änderungen überwachen Sie werden über jede verkündete oder in Kraft tretende Änderung per Mail informiert, sofort, wöchentlich oder in dem Intervall, das Sie gewählt haben. Auf Wunsch werden Sie zusätzlich im konfigurierten Abstand vor Inkrafttreten erinnert. Stellen Sie Ihr Paket zu überwachender Vorschriften beliebig zusammen. Rechtskataster - Jetzt anmelden! perturbation.justification: The change from 'reasonable best efforts' to a standard deemed sufficient by the disclosing party significantly weakens the protection of confidential information. The Law Explanation states that § 4 of the German Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The modified text could allow a disclosing party to set a very low standard of care, potentially falling short of the 'reasonable measures' standard imposed by law. Although there are no directly relevant scraped law snippets, the change in the contract text, in conjunction with the Law Explanation, creates a situation where the contract could fail to meet the legal standard of 'reasonable measures' for trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the termination of the business relationship or upon request, whichever is sooner, each party shall return all of the other's Conﬁdential Information, certifying destruction of any copies or partial copies made.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the termination of the business relationship or upon request, whichever is sooner, each party shall return the other's Conﬁdential Information, without a requirement to certify destruction of copies or partial copies made.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement to certify destruction undermines the assurance that confidential information is no longer retained. This affects compliance with data protection laws and potentially intellectual property laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: GDPR Article 17 concerns the right to erasure ('right to be forgotten'). While returning the information is a step, without certifying destruction, it's impossible to ensure compliance with the obligation to erase personal data where no longer necessary, especially when copies may exist.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Art. 17 GDPR – Right to erasure (‘right to be forgotten’) - General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) Search for: Skip to content Search for: Imprint | Privacy Policy | Liability The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay where one of the following grounds applies: the personal data are no longer necessary in relation to the purposes for which they were collected or otherwise processed; the data subject withdraws consent on which the processing is based according to point (a) of Article 6 (1), or point (a) of Article 9 (2), and where there is no other legal ground for the processing; the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (1) and there are no overriding legitimate grounds for the processing, or the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21 (2); the personal data have been unlawfully processed; the personal data have to be erased for compliance with a legal obligation in Union or Member State law to which the controller is subject; the personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of information society services referred to in Article 8 (1). Where the controller has made the personal data public and is obliged pursuant to paragraph 1 to erase the personal data, the controller, taking account of available technology and the cost of implementation, shall take reasonable steps, including technical measures, to inform controllers which are processing the personal data that the data subject has requested the erasure by such controllers of any links to, or copy or replication of, those personal data. Paragraphs 1 and 2 shall not apply to the extent that processing is necessary: for exercising the right of freedom of expression and information; for compliance with a legal obligation which requires processing by Union or Member State law to w
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the certification requirement for the destruction of confidential information, which contradicts the GDPR's mandate for data erasure. Article 17 of the GDPR, as seen in Scraped Law Snippet 2, grants data subjects the 'right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay and the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay'. Without a certification of destruction, it is impossible to ensure that personal data has been completely erased, particularly in cases where copies have been made, creating a situation where the controller cannot guarantee compliance with GDPR's data erasure requirements. The original text ensured compliance but the changed text does not.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is governed by the laws of Germany without regard to its rules on conﬂicts of law, and both parties consent to the venue and jurisdiction of its courts.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is governed by the laws of Germany, and both parties consent to the venue and jurisdiction of the courts of Liechtenstein.
perturbation.explanation: By specifying the courts of Liechtenstein, while stating the agreement is governed by the laws of Germany, creates a conflict of laws issue. Enforcing a German law contract in Liechtenstein courts introduces uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 6 of the Rome I Regulation concerns individual employment contracts. It stipulates that the law chosen by the parties shall not deprive the employee of the protection afforded to him by provisions that cannot be derogated from by agreement under the law of the country where the employee habitually carries out his work. Specifying a foreign court raises questions regarding the proper application of these mandatory rules.

perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies the courts of Liechtenstein while stating the agreement is governed by the laws of Germany. This creates a conflict because enforcing a German law contract in Liechtenstein courts introduces uncertainty and potential issues related to the interpretation and application of German law in a foreign legal system. The Law Explanation mentions Article 6 of the Rome I Regulation, highlighting concerns about mandatory rules. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions the Rome I Regulation (EC No 593/2008), it primarily points to the regulation's existence and availability across multiple languages, it doesn't explicitly detail how the conflict between German law and Liechtenstein courts directly violates a specific article, but supports the potential conflict. Snippet 2 was not helpful. This creates a misaligned terminology contradiction, which arises when contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Each party may disclose Conﬁdential Information only on a need-to-know basis to its own employees and to its consultants under appropriate written burden of conﬁdentiality. Each party shall take appropriate action with its employees and advisors to satisfy its obligations under this Agreement. Each party shall be responsible to the other for any violation of this Agreement by its own employees or consultants.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party may disclose Conﬁdential Information to its employees and consultants. Each party shall take appropriate action with its employees and advisors to satisfy its obligations under this Agreement. Each party shall not be responsible to the other for any violation of this Agreement by its own employees or consultants unless such violation was intentional.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'need-to-know' restriction and limiting liability for employee/consultant breaches to intentional violations significantly weakens the protection of Confidential Information. This may not comply with the standard of care required to protect trade secrets under German law.
perturbation.law_explanation: § 5 of the German Trade Secrets Act defines the conditions for the unlawful acquisition, use, and disclosure of trade secrets. By removing the 'need-to-know' basis and limiting liability to intentional breaches, the contract potentially permits actions that would be considered unlawful under the GeschGehG, such as negligent or reckless disclosure of trade secrets by employees.

perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the protection of confidential information by removing the 'need-to-know' basis and limiting liability for breaches to intentional violations. The Law Explanation says that this potentially permits actions that would be considered unlawful under the GeschGehG (German Trade Secrets Act), such as negligent or reckless disclosure of trade secrets by employees. Although the scraped snippets are not directly relevant, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation support that this change introduces a significant weakening of trade secret protection, potentially violating German law, thus creating a legal contradiction as defined by the 'Misaligned Terminology' perturbation type.
